subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The entire chapter is organised around the idea of percentage — defining it as a fraction with denominator 100 (section 1.1), computing percentage of a quantity and percentages greater than 100 (section 1.2), and applying it to comparison, increase/decrease, profit/loss, taxes, and compound interest (section 1.3). All 53 exercises directly engage this competency.

The chapter continuously works across the FDP (Fractions–Decimals–Percentages) trio: section 1.1 converts fractions to percentages via equivalent fractions and cross-multiplication; section 1.2 uses ratios (millet:water = 2:7) to derive percentage composition; and section 1.3 moves fluidly between decimal multipliers (0.82×CP for 18% loss) and percentage expressions throughout profit/loss and interest calculations.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Several exercises require posing and solving a linear equation to find an unknown: E-26 (a number increased by 20% becomes 90 → 1.2n = 90), E-23 (reverse-calculate original price from a discounted amount), and WE-9/WE-13 (find total distance or original cost price from a given percentage and amount). These go beyond applying a known percentage formula to setting up proportional equations.

The chapter repeatedly asks students to construct and evaluate logical arguments about percentage claims: E-45 requires algebraic justification that x% of y equals y% of x; E-43 asks students to reason (not compute) about whether a 35% margin + 30% discount results in profit or loss; and WE-24 (Surbhi mishap) and WE-23 (compound discount) refute intuitive but incorrect claims using deductive reasoning.

The compound interest derivation in WE-18 through WE-20 is built through explicit iteration: the ending amount of Year 1 becomes the principal for Year 2, and so on, leading to the generalised formula p(1+r)^t through symbolic abstraction. E-36 (Math Talk on doubling time) and E-42 (sequential percentage changes on a population) further require iterative/algorithmic reasoning about compounding.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to interpret, convert, and apply percentages — the central idea of CG-1's C-1.5 and C-1.6 — by grounding the fraction-to-percentage relationship in proportional reasoning and then systematically extending it to real-world quantity comparisons, commercial transactions, and exponential growth through compounding.
